methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $640M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $6.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Redwood Materials
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series D), post-money $6B+

data.narrative:
Solugen is a Series D climate and energy company founded by Gaurab Chakrabarti and Sean Hunt. The valuation analysis draws on a peer set of four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage private companies (Redwood Materials, KoBold Metals, Electric Hydrogen, and Amogy), alongside one public comparable.

Two triangulation methods were applied. The stage-progression method uses Solugen's $640M in total raised as a proxy baseline, applying a typical 1.5x Series D step-up multiplier to arrive at $960M. The peer-median method benchmarks against four directly comparable private companies with valuations ranging from $700M (Amogy, Series C) to $6.0B (Redwood Materials, Series D), yielding a median valuation of $1.5B. The wide peer range reflects the heterogeneity within frontier-climate financing.

These methods triangulate to a valuation range of $816M to $1.7B, with a median of $1.2B. The gap between the stage-progression result ($960M) and peer median ($1.5B) reflects uncertainty in relative company maturity and market position within the cohort. Key uncertainties include the materiality of Solugen's differentiated technology relative to Redwood's significantly higher valuation, and the degree to which recent market conditions have compressed or expanded multiples in the private climate space. The peer range itself—spanning one order of magnitude—underscores valuation sensitivity to stage classification and perceived commercialization risk.
